lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market converters and integrated manufacturers that repeatedly supply plastic film bags or pouches to external food, consumer-products, healthcare, industrial, retail, waste, or e-commerce customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive packaging operations, pure distributors, manufacturers only of film or sheet without bag or pouch conversion, paper-combination bags classified elsewhere, purchased-material printers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring per-unit orders and supply programs for stock or custom bags and pouches, often involving print, dimensions, barrier, seal, gauge, and qualification specifications; revenue can range from spot commodity orders to negotiated multi-year programs.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the broader 326100 plastics-products workforce, not wage-weighted task exposure in 326111. Its mix of extruding, forming, packaging, inspection, maintenance, supervision, logistics, sales, and office roles is translated into an AI-exposed share: planning, vision-inspection triage, predictive maintenance, quoting, artwork workflow, procurement, customer service, and administration are exposed, while machine tending and material handling largely are not. The broader mix may overstate exposure if bag plants are more production-intensive, or understate it where custom printed pouches require more prepress and technical service.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is dominated by equipment-centered production, but a meaningful minority of wage cost sits in information-heavy planning, quality, engineering, commercial, and administrative tasks. The range excludes already-automated machine motion and counts only not-yet-automated work.
a.change_evidence: A 326111-specific staffing and payroll extract matched to direct observation of extrusion, converting, printing, inspection, maintenance, scheduling, artwork, quality, warehousing, and office task hours and current automation.
caveats: The occupation source is four-digit plastics manufacturing, not this six-digit industry.
Employment shares are not task shares and require judgmental wage weighting.

rho.rationale: Machine vision, schedule optimization, predictive-maintenance support, document automation, quoting, and customer-service copilots can be deployed within five years. Realization is constrained by legacy converters, sensor coverage, short runs, material variability, food or medical qualification, false-reject costs, worker adoption, and the need to retain accountable setup and release decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operating data from LMM bag and pouch plants implementing vision, scheduling, maintenance, and workflow AI, showing net labor hours avoided, scrap, uptime, complaints, and payback after twelve months.
caveats: BLS documents automation in machine operation generally, not AI implementation outcomes.
Implementation may improve throughput or scrap without reducing labor.

e.rationale: Many independent converters sell repeat external packaging and have transferable equipment, customer programs, and operating teams. Eligibility is reduced by captive plants, thin-margin commodity bags, customer concentration, single-use regulatory exposure, lease or equipment constraints, and businesses whose economics depend on a founder's selling relationships.
e.change_evidence: A complete 326111 firm roster with EBITDA, ownership, captive status, revenue recurrence, end-market and customer concentration, equipment age and liens, resin pass-through terms, environmental permits, and product qualification portability.
caveats: Flexible packaging sources include activities outside plastic bags and pouches.

s5.bridge: Census ABS age-of-owner tables cover employer businesses broadly rather than qualifying control transfers among 326111 LMM manufacturers. The estimate translates general succession exposure into a five-year probability, discounting family transfers, closures, corporate divisions, recapitalizations without control change, and operations that do not survive diligence. It may overstate a consolidating corporate-owned sector or understate mature founder-owned converters.
s5.rationale: A sizeable independent-converter population and general owner succession support recurring transaction potential, but owner age is not observed deal flow and no complete six-digit transfer dataset was found.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, broker, and bankruptcy transactions mapped to a 326111 roster and classified by control, EBITDA band, operating continuity, and buyer type.
caveats: ABS includes other industries and sizes.
Plant sales and internal restructurings are not necessarily qualifying firm transfers.

q.rationale: Savings can be retained during fixed-price periods and are better protected in custom printed, barrier, validated, or short-run pouches with switching friction. Commodity bags, resin-indexed pricing, annual bids, transparent cost structures, and concentrated consumer or retail customers can pass benefits through quickly.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice cohorts separating resin indices, cost-plus and fixed-price terms, bid frequency, renewal cadence, rebates, customer concentration, qualification cost, and price realization following documented productivity gains.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-generated savings.
Value capture varies materially between commodity sacks and differentiated printed or barrier pouches.

d5.bridge: Census retail e-commerce growth and the Flexible Packaging Association's broad industry footprint measure demand-adjacent activity, not constant-quality 326111 physical units. E-commerce, food, healthcare, and industrial shipping support bag and pouch use, while EPA's plastic-pollution strategy signals pressure on single-use items. Translating those opposing forces to a five-year industry quantity may overstate growth if reuse, bans, lightweighting, or paper substitution accelerate, or understate it if pouches continue gaining share from rigid formats.
d5.rationale: Flexible bags and pouches serve durable packaging functions across diverse end markets, and e-commerce remains a supportive channel. Single-use reduction, extended producer responsibility, recycled-content rules, material substitution, and lightweighting create a credible contraction case. The base is modest real quantity growth rather than extrapolation of nominal sales.
d5.change_evidence: Census product-class shipment volumes by bag and pouch type and resin, customer-sector packaging-unit forecasts, state EPR and bag-ban compliance data, and measured substitution to paper, reusable, compostable, or rigid packaging.
caveats: E-commerce sales are not packaging units and include digital services and goods with varied packaging.
The trade association covers flexible packaging beyond this NAICS code.
EPA strategy statements do not establish enacted nationwide quantity limits.

o.rationale: Surviving demand for a physical bag or pouch still requires extrusion or film sourcing, converting, sealing, printing, inspection, and accountable delivery. Software may reduce packaging demand or enable customer procurement self-service, but it cannot supply the physical protective format; insourcing and material substitution are the larger operator risks.
o.change_evidence: Customer packaging roadmaps and procurement records identifying volumes eliminated, converted to reusable formats, insourced, or moved to alternative materials, plus evidence of on-site autonomous bag-making systems.
caveats: This is conditional on year-five demand remaining after reduction and substitution.
An external operator may lose volume to vertical integration even when physical demand persists.

narrative.executive_view: Plastic bag and pouch manufacturing combines recurring physical packaging demand with a machine-intensive workforce. AI is most credible in scheduling, quality triage, maintenance, artwork and order workflow, quoting, and service rather than direct replacement of converting labor. Custom and validated formats can retain value, while commodity bags face bid pressure and policy-driven substitution.
narrative.ai_impact: Extrusion, converting, sealing, printing, material handling, setup, and maintenance remain physical. Machine vision, anomaly detection, schedule and resin optimization, predictive-maintenance support, artwork review, document extraction, quoting, and customer-service automation can reduce indirect and exception-handling work.
narrative.value_capture: Custom graphics, barrier performance, food or medical qualification, and reliable short-run service provide switching friction. Commodity grocery, merchandise, trash, or simple industrial bags are more transparent and frequently bid, while resin-index clauses can quickly expose lower costs to customers.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry likely contains transferable independent converters, but the nominal pool must be filtered for captive operations, obsolete assets, customer concentration, regulatory exposure, thin margins, and founder-dependent selling. General owner-age evidence suggests succession, not a measured deal probability.
narrative.demand_durability: Food, healthcare, waste handling, industrial shipping, and e-commerce sustain packaging functions, and flexible formats can displace heavier rigid packaging. Offsetting forces include lightweighting, reuse, paper substitution, bag restrictions, extended producer responsibility, and pressure to reduce single-use plastics.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risks are resin and energy volatility, customer concentration, commodity bids, food-contact or medical qualification, print and seal quality, equipment reliability, recyclability requirements, and fast-changing plastic policy. Broad flexible-packaging and e-commerce indicators are imperfect quantity proxies.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring demand for lightweight physical packaging supports repeat customer programs and targeted AI improvements around production and commercial workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Commodity pricing and accelerating single-use-plastic reduction or material substitution can erode both demand and retained savings.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Plastics Bag and Pouch Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines converting resins or film into single- or multiweb plastic bags and pouches and identifies relevant exclusions.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Plastics Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Provides the broader plastics-manufacturing occupation mix, including extruding, forming, inspection, packaging, and other production roles used as the task-mix proxy.

sources.title: Metal and Plastic Machine Workers
sources.supports: Describes setup, test runs, minor repairs, inspection, monitoring, and how automation can let operators control multiple machines.

sources.title: ABS Characteristics of Business Owners: 2022 Tables
sources.supports: Provides age-of-owner tables used as a broad succession proxy.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey Methodology: 2022
sources.supports: Explains ABS coverage, sampling, and business and owner characteristic limitations.

sources.title: Quarterly Retail E-Commerce Sales: First Quarter 2026
sources.supports: Reports $326.7 billion of seasonally adjusted first-quarter 2026 e-commerce sales, 9.8% above first-quarter 2025 and 16.9% of total retail sales, as a packaging-demand proxy.

sources.title: U.S. Flexible Packaging Industry Economic Impact
sources.supports: Describes flexible packaging's role across food, healthcare, agriculture, and e-commerce and reports its broad 2025 US economic footprint.

sources.title: National Strategy to Prevent Plastic Pollution
sources.supports: Documents policy directions including reducing single-use plastic consumption, expanding reuse and refill, and considering producer-responsibility approaches.
